name: Ama-no-Murakumo
description: Ama-no-Murakumo, later known as Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (草薙の剣, 'Grass-Cutting Sword'), is a legendary Japanese sword and one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan. Its origin is steeped in myth, tracing back to the storm god Susanoo. After being banished from the heavens, Susanoo encountered a grieving family tormented by the fearsome Yamata no Orochi, an eight-headed serpent. Susanoo cleverly intoxicated the serpent and, in its weakened state, slayed it. Inside the serpent's tail, he discovered a magnificent sword, which he then presented to his sister, the sun goddess Amaterasu, as a gesture of reconciliation. The sword eventually passed into the hands of the Yamato Imperial family, solidifying its role as a symbol of imperial power and divine right. Its power is not merely in its sharpness but also in its association with courage, protection, and the ability to overcome great challenges. The sword is said to possess the ability to control the wind and is believed to be capable of warding off evil. The Kusanagi is currently enshrined at the Atsuta Shrine in Nagoya, although it is never publicly displayed. Its presence is a constant reminder of the divine origins and enduring strength of the Japanese imperial line, representing a potent link between the earthly and spiritual realms. The sword's absence from public view only adds to its mystique and reinforces its sacred nature. It is more than just a weapon; it is a national treasure and a powerful symbol of Japanese identity.
subtitle: The Grass-Cutting Sword of Legend
origin: Found inside the tail of the Yamata no Orochi by Susanoo, gifted to Amaterasu.

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Yamata no Orochi
keyMyths.description: Susanoo slays the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi and discovers the sword within its tail. He presents the sword to Amaterasu as a peace offering.

keyMyths.title: Yamato Takeru and the Burning Field
keyMyths.description: Prince Yamato Takeru used the Kusanagi to cut away the grass and set a counter-fire to escape a burning field. He realized the sword's power to control the wind in doing so.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: Translation of the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.
